speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 15, 1995. We're interviewing Esther Bankier, nee, Kramer. My name is Sheila Rodin-Novak. We're conducting the interview in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, in the United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English. My name is Sheila Rodin-Novak. We're conducting an interview today with Esther Bankier, in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. Today is November 15, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Bankier, we want to thank you for letting us interview you today. Could you please tell us what your name is and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Bankier-- E-S-T-H-E-R, B-A-N-K-I-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-A-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 24, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age is?

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you tell us the city and country of your birth? And spell them for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Magierow-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to-- M-A-G-E-R-O-W. And what country is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you tell us a little bit about what your life was like as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I come from a very well-to-do family. We always had a maid in the house and a nice house. And my father had business. It was very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a-- a mill, you know, where you make flour and all different kinds. It was a family enterprise. It used to be from generation to generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many sisters and brothers did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had three sisters and one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was a good child. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are some of your best memories as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always very happy, very content. And I was very young when the war started, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you do? What was your daily life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. I belonged to a Zionist organization. I learned Hebrew. You know, it was a nice town, where I come. So, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends? A lot of friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, oh, yes. You know, it was-- it was a lot of Jews in our town. And everybody was very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how your parents met?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your educational background?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was public school. And it was high school-- a few years high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the same town where-- in the high school, I went in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in Lemberg during the week, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- during the school year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come home at weekends, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh, yes. It was not far. It was not far.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you stay with in Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what your home life was like-- what you-- the family routine was normally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know how to-- it was a nice family-- children, you know. My mother was a housekeeper. She was very educated. She had one year university. But then they didn't let her go no more. See, Pole and Jews were not allowed to go to the university. But otherwise, she was a housekeeper. And we were pretty well to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, you-- she had her friends, I suppose. I don't remember too much. You know? I didn't understood--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --too-- too much to pay attention to it. And did you have any of your grandparents living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I had grandparents. But they didn't live with me. They lived separate. We had a nice family. You know, you visit, you-- the holidays, you spend together. You know, it was a typical European town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty many.

speaker: interviewer
text: On both your parents' side, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side. My father was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many siblings did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: She just had one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a relationship did you have with your sisters and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were little. You know, I was the oldest, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your religious background like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Europe, everybody was religious. You know, you didn't know not to be religious. Everybody was orthodox, most.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: We belonged to temple, sure we-- everybody was active. That was the European style.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family orthodox, or more traditional?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox, orthodox. There was not-- I mean, in my town, everybody was orthodox. We didn't have like here, you know, of individuals.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many synagogues were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was ultra-orthodox. And one was a little more modern. And my father belonged to both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends where the parents went. You know, kids, we used to play around the temple. That what you do Saturday or the holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish community like? Was it a big community? Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not too big of a town. But the community was very close. Everybody knew everybody. Everybody participate together whatever occasion was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the holidays like? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the holidays were beautiful-- very observant and very nice. Everybody observed the-- very much. I'm still lonesome for-- for it. It was kind. It wasn't like here, you know, to pretend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most of the people come to your house for the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends. Usually, you know, you eat dinner. And then you go visiting. So, you know, you exchange things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family do a lot of things? I mean, did they sing or do things together, go on vacations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your family's relationship to the non-Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had neighbors. And it was pretty nice. You know, at that time, in my time, we didn't know-- everybody respect everybody. There was no divisions like here. You know, things. But everybody lived in-- in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a friendly relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very friendly. Very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- Oh yes. We respect each other. We used to respect. The gentiles and the-- and the gentiles used to respect the Jews very much. See, it's in the war, everything happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have non-Jewish friends that you played with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Oh yes, because we went to school together. There wasn't a separate school for Jews or non-Jews. We all went to school together, and you mix up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Lemberg, was it the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lemberg was a big city, a very big city. That was more modern. You know, it was a different type of-- but in the smaller towns, it was different too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your life like in Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was so young. And not too much-- you go to school. And the weekends, I used to come home. And there wasn't too much time I was there-- for two years, so. I really remember it had an opera house and had a lot of synagogues. It had orthodox and not-so orthodox. It was a different type of life there, which in my time, I didn't understood. You know, I could evaluate-- now I would. But, you know, when you're young-- and you don't pay attention to things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to do different things in Lemberg? Did you go to the theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, once in a while. And there was movie houses, sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any experiences of anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg. In Lemberg, it started. It was not far from the war. And you know, the gentiles-- it was pretty bad-- but not in our town, not where I came from. You know, you go, and they used to throw a pins on you-- the gentiles, you know. You go for a walk, or-- and it was pretty bad. Just, I don't know. I think maybe they start the anti-Semitism, which I didn't realize at the time what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because there was a incident where they killed two Jewish students in the university. And they come-- came from a small town. And their parents said, why?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't understand why they were killed. And they were killed because they were Jews. And that's how that started. It was the year before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were some of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, you know, I think I was stupid. I didn't realize. You know, I didn't-- like now, I could understand. But at the time, I wondered too, why? Why do they throw pins in the back, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How could they knew I'm not gentile? You know? Or they used to smash windows in the big stores. So I was wondering too, what-- but I didn't realize what it was. When--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was very shortly before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Kristallnacht happened, did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany. That wasn't in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no repercussions in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- that was not in Poland. That was already when the Russian took over, where I come from. But Germany, the other part of Poland belonged to Germany. See?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- it was different. And it was far away. And that's why we always thought it happened there. But it could never happen-- move here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you hear any rumors about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was rumors, yes. As a matter of fact, we had, in the family, some musicians in Berlin. And they came. You know, they ran away from Hitler. And they came. And we begged them to stay. And they said-- they ran away. They-- they advised us to run away too. But like I said, nobody believed something like this could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we heard rumors. And people used to come over. But they never stayed. They used to move further to Russia. And that's when the trouble started. How did your parents react to these rumors? Did they say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was so short. You know, a year is not so-- so, you know, you-- you-- till you realize what really happened, it happened. And then I wasn't with them. Because I was in school, and they evacuated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I really didn't know what-- what-- what it really-- how bad, really, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in September of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939? I was still-- it was Russia. The part where I came belonged to Russia. It wasn't too bad. You know, they took away the business. And my father, it wasn't in my father's name. It was a family enterprise. So they let them work. And people managed. It was hard, but they managed. And then it wasn't that long neither. It was-- it was start to be bad. But it wasn't as bad as when Hitler came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's talk about a little bit how your life changed once it became part of Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's changed a lot. First of all, they took away the business. And a lot of people couldn't make a living. You have to help each other. We used to help. I used to go, middle of the night, and my father used to give me some flour. And then I used to take it to a lady who used to bake bread. And people used to come and get the bread, because there was-- you know, the stores weren't there or nothing. So we-- we feel the pinch. But everybody thought it's temporarily, it's not going to be forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And did your neighbors help one another? Did everybody-- was everybody able to work who chose to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it mostly was gentiles. They had their own fields, their own gardens. It was still-- where I came from, it was quiet. In the big cities, it was problem. But where I came, it was-- like I said-- people managed. And people thought it's temporarily. You know, the slogan was-- the Russian-- you know-- there were-- you-- if you were rich, they took it away and they give it to the poor, or things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for some people, it was a blessing. And some people were in trouble. But there was not too much time for me to realize whatever-- what-- what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in '41, the Germans came. And I wasn't there. I was in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back before we go into '41. Did you start to see signs of anti-Semitism in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. I was in school in Lemberg. And-- you know, you go to school. You come home. It was hard. But like I said, you manage. You get used to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have no choice. Because the people who came from Germany told us to run. But what is there afraid you to run? Nobody realized. You know, you have to get used to the situation. Did you start to hear anything about concentration camps, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not that time, no. Not in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was it like under the Russians? I mean, did-- did-- was the Russian army in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. You see, the town I lived, they made a border between Germany and Russia. I mean, they divided Poland in two. So we belonged to the Russian, and the other one belonged to the Germans. And it-- like when I had to come home from Lemberg, I had to have a-- a card. You couldn't go just to go. Because there was the border, not far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the Russians respond to the Jewish people in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice. At that time, they were not anti-Semitic. I couldn't say. I was three years in Russia. They didn't understand, you know. Everything happened after the war, whatever happened. But you know, the-- they were the same, like, for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still Stalin there, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- you were in school. Now, this was a bigger town. Is Lemberg the same as Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what it is-- Lemberg, Lwow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lemberg and Lwow is the same. And tell me how--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --things changed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was, there was the Russian. So everybody lived their own life. Because the-- it was a change, because they closed down the business, the big business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somehow people managed. They didn't know what to expect. See? It was all of a sudden. Did you notice, after the Russians came in, any big changes in Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at the time, not at the time. They were very nice to the people. They were very nice to the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were in school. And then in 1941, tell us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it was in June, you know. We-- I-- we had exams. And I told you, you know, I got that-- when they-- I got an award to being a good student. And I came home. And it was, like, a weekend. And then that Sunday-- I don't remember what it was. It was from the school. They told us to come behind the city and dig trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, all the young people went. And while we were digging, they start to bombard. But we didn't know what it was. So then the-- the man who was in charge, came and said, stop digging. And we were afraid. We thought maybe we didn't do a good job. You know, in Russia, if you don't mind, you get punished. So we told him, you know, we're going to behave, and we're going to do a better job. He said, no, no, no. Come on. And then trucks came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they put us on the trucks. And they said, we-- we have to evacuate you. And that was the last time I saw my parents, in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were at home in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they gathered up all the--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the young people to dig the trenches. So I suppose, maybe, the authorities knew what was going on. But they wouldn't tell us. And while we were driving, we heard bomb-- you know, it was such a panic. People didn't know what-- what happened and what to expect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were driving almost the whole day. And then we drove to a different town where they have the train. And they would bombarding all the time. So some people didn't want to drive. So they went from the trucks. And they said, they going to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it happens I was staying on the truck. And then while we were driving, I met two more of my friends. And we decided whatever happened, we're going to be together. And then we came to a city-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- no. I forgot the name-- where there was the train. And the Russian said we should-- who wants to be evacuated should go on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went on the train. Until people packed on, on the train, they start to bombard again. So then-- then it was-- it was terrible, because some got killed. Some, you know, and some from the train-- the-- what do you call it? The-- They disconnected, and they connected the-- the part from the train. How do you call them? The cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cars. And-- it was-- and they said, they going to evacuated us. So we were driving-- I don't know-- for a day, for two days, till we--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, they took you off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them took, because they bombard some of the cars. So then when-- when they came in the morning, we were near Kiev, in Russia. In the meantime, a lot of people went back. They didn't want to go. And it just happened I was-- we-- three of us were together. And we stick together. And then Stalin give-- said, we should-- everybody, the train's going to be free. You should go wherever you want in Russia. And they used to give food on the stations. They were very helpful. So we took a train to Kharkov. It's a differ-- it's a big city in Russia. And we went down. I mean, we went down, and we-- we had some papers. They give us some papers while they evacuated us. And in Kharkov, we went to a-- you know, they have some little towns. We went to a town. And we stayed there. And the Germans were not yet in Kiev. But they knew the war started already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we worked there. We took care of cows. We work in the fields, whatever they told us. And we had food and shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there maybe two months. And then we saw the German planes flying. But nobody knew. You know, that time, they didn't know a different Jew or not Jew. But we know who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we-- we went further. And that was for three years. If you saw the Germans, we moved. We always were not far-- about 5, 10 kilometer from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then eventually, you know, after a year, the Russian start the offensive. And then when the German had a bad time in Stalingrad-- I don't know how they call it now. So, you see, the Russians start to push them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how to-- and we lived there. And we worked in the fields. You know? They used to have sugar factories. So we used to raise sugar beets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- so we exist. You know what I mean. We made-- we had the food. That's all what we had-- till-- till the war ended-- not the war didn't ended-- till they pushed back the Germans to Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they took over the-- the part for-- where the Russian were-- not the whole part, but part of the part-- we start to go back. OK, we're going to stop here. We have to change the--
